enrichment.short_description: Dext is a cloud bookkeeping automation platform that captures and extracts data from receipts, invoices, and bank statements for accountants and small businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: Dext Review 2026: Pricing, Features & Bookkeeping Automation
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dext automates receipt capture and bookkeeping data entry for accountants and small businesses. See 2026 pricing, features, integrations, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dext (formerly Receipt Bank, later Dext Prepare) is a cloud-based bookkeeping automation and financial document management platform built primarily for accountants, bookkeepers, and the small businesses they serve. Founded in 2010 and headquartered in London, United Kingdom, the company built its reputation by solving one of the most repetitive parts of small-business accounting: manually keying data from paper and digital receipts, invoices, and bank statements into accounting software. Dext's core product uses optical character recognition and machine-learning-based data extraction to read scanned or photographed documents, automatically identify line items, supplier names, dates, tax amounts, and totals, and then categorize and publish that data directly into cloud accounting platforms such as QuickBooks Online, Xero, Sage, and FreeAgent. The company states its extraction engine processes several hundred million documents per year with claimed data accuracy above 99 percent, and that its customer base spans more than 700,000 businesses and over 12,000 accounting and bookkeeping firms globally, making it one of the most widely deployed receipt-capture tools in the practice-management ecosystem alongside competitors like Hubdoc (owned by Xero), AutoEntry, and expense tools such as Expensify.

Dext's product suite has grown beyond simple receipt scanning. Dext Prepare, the historical core product, handles document capture via a mobile app, email forwarding, a browser extension, and a dedicated client portal where an accounting firm's customers can upload documents directly. Dext Commerce, an add-on, automatically pulls sales and fee data from e-commerce and payment platforms such as Amazon, Shopify, PayPal, Stripe, and Etsy, which is particularly useful for bookkeepers servicing online sellers. Dext Precision, aimed at accounting-firm operations, adds practice-management-style monitoring of bookkeeping quality and client-data health across a firm's entire client roster. The unifying idea across the suite is eliminating manual data entry and reducing time spent on write-up work so accountants can focus on higher-value advisory services.

Commercially, Dext runs a subscription model priced primarily by number of users and monthly document volume, split into two plan families: a Business plan for companies managing their own books, and a Practice plan for accounting and bookkeeping firms managing multiple clients. The Business plan starts at roughly $31.50 per month billed monthly, or about $25.21 per month billed annually, for a tier including five users and 250 documents, with usage-based add-ons and higher tiers scaling from there. Once a plan's monthly document allowance is reached, uploads are still accepted but automatic extraction pauses until the next billing cycle or an upgrade. The Practice plan starts at roughly $239 per month for a bundle covering ten business clients, with per-client pricing scaling for larger firms. A free trial is available on both families, and custom pricing is offered for high-volume or complex needs; Dext does not publish a permanently free tier.

Ownership has changed hands several times. Dext was originally venture-backed, raising more than $140 million from investors including Insight Partners, Kennet Partners, Hg, Augmentum Fintech, and CIBC. In December 2024, IRIS Software Group, a UK-based accounting, payroll, and compliance software group, acquired Dext and folded it into its broader portfolio of practice-management and cloud accounting tools while continuing to operate it as a distinct product and brand. Recent company profiles put Dext's headcount at roughly 500 employees globally.

Dext's key differentiators versus competitors are its extraction-accuracy claims, breadth of accounting-software integrations, and its narrow focus on the accountant and bookkeeper workflow rather than being a generic expense-management tool for end-user employees, which distinguishes it from consumer-facing spend tools. Its mobile apps for iOS and Android let clients photograph receipts on the go, and its browser extension plus a unique per-user forwarding email address make document capture nearly frictionless for non-technical small-business owners. Dext also exposes an API for firms and software vendors that want to build custom integrations into its extraction pipeline.

Target users fall into two groups: accounting and bookkeeping firms that use Dext Practice to standardize and speed up write-up work across dozens or hundreds of clients, and small and mid-sized businesses that use Dext Business directly to keep their own books tidy without hiring a full-time bookkeeper. Within the cloud-accounting ecosystem, Dext is frequently bundled or recommended alongside Xero and QuickBooks Online as part of a best-in-class app stack for modern accounting practices.

Common praise from reviewers on platforms like G2 and Capterra centers on time savings, extraction accuracy, and ease of use for non-accountants submitting receipts; frequent criticism centers on pricing that scales quickly for firms with many clients or high document volumes, occasional misreads on low-quality images, and a learning curve when configuring supplier rules and chart-of-account mappings. On security and compliance, Dext emphasizes encrypted data handling and GDPR compliance given its UK and EU customer base, and it offers in-app chat support, an extensive help center, and dedicated account management for larger Practice-tier firms. Regular product updates, such as expanded bank-statement extraction and supplier-statement reconciliation, continue to narrow the gap between what a business spent and what appears on its bank feed. Dext remains one of the category-defining products in receipt-capture and bookkeeping automation, a category it helped establish under its original Receipt Bank branding before rebranding to Dext in 2021.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dext is a cloud bookkeeping automation platform that eliminates manual data entry by extracting information from receipts, invoices, bank statements, and e-commerce sales data, then publishing it directly into accounting software.

Originally launched as Receipt Bank in 2010 and rebranded to Dext in 2021, the London-based company serves both accounting firms managing many clients and individual small businesses managing their own books.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Dext's OCR and machine-learning extraction engine reads photographed or scanned documents and identifies suppliers, line items, dates, and totals with claimed accuracy above 99 percent.

Add-on modules like Dext Commerce pull sales and fee data automatically from platforms such as Amazon, Shopify, and Stripe, while Dext Precision gives firms visibility into bookkeeping quality across their whole client base.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Dext is priced by user count and monthly document volume across two plan families: Business, for companies managing their own books, and Practice, for accounting firms managing multiple clients.

Business plans start around $31.50/month (about $25.21/month billed annually), while Practice plans start around $239/month for ten business clients; a free trial is available and custom pricing exists for high-volume needs.

features.title: Automated data extraction
features.description: OCR and machine learning read receipts, invoices, and bank statements to extract supplier, date, and amount data automatically.

features.title: Accounting software integrations
features.description: Publishes extracted data directly into Xero, QuickBooks Online, Sage, and FreeAgent.

features.title: Mobile capture apps
features.description: iOS and Android apps let clients photograph receipts on the go for instant processing.

features.title: Email forwarding inbox
features.description: Each user gets a unique email address to forward invoices and receipts for automatic capture.

features.title: Dext Commerce
features.description: Automatically imports sales, fees, and payout data from e-commerce and payment platforms like Amazon, Shopify, and Stripe.

features.title: Client portal
features.description: A dedicated portal lets accounting firm clients upload documents directly into the firm's workflow.

features.title: Dext Precision
features.description: Practice-management monitoring that tracks bookkeeping quality and data health across a firm's client roster.

features.title: API access
features.description: Exposes an API for firms and software vendors to build custom integrations into the extraction pipeline.

pros: High-accuracy automated data extraction saves significant manual entry time
Broad integration coverage with leading cloud accounting platforms
Simple mobile and email capture flow that non-technical clients can use easily
Purpose-built for the accountant/bookkeeper workflow rather than generic expense tracking
cons: Pricing scales quickly for firms with many clients or high document volumes
Occasional extraction errors on low-quality or handwritten receipts
Learning curve when configuring supplier rules and chart-of-account mappings
No permanent free tier, only a time-limited free trial

faqs.question: What is Dext used for?
faqs.answer: Dext automates the capture and data extraction of receipts, invoices, and bank statements so accountants and small businesses can avoid manual bookkeeping data entry.

faqs.question: How much does Dext cost?
faqs.answer: Dext's Business plan starts around $31.50/month (about $25.21/month billed annually) for 5 users and 250 documents, while the Practice plan for accounting firms starts around $239/month for 10 clients.

faqs.question: Does Dext have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Dext does not offer a permanent free plan, but it does offer a free trial on both its Business and Practice plans.

faqs.question: Which accounting software does Dext integrate with?
faqs.answer: Dext integrates with major cloud accounting platforms including Xero, QuickBooks Online, Sage, and FreeAgent.

faqs.question: Who owns Dext?
faqs.answer: Dext was acquired by IRIS Software Group, a UK-based accounting and compliance software company, in December 2024.

faqs.question: Is Dext the same as Receipt Bank?
faqs.answer: Yes, Dext was originally launched as Receipt Bank in 2010 and rebranded to Dext in 2021.

use_cases.title: Accounting firm write-up work
use_cases.description: Firms use Dext Practice to collect, extract, and code documents across dozens or hundreds of clients, speeding up monthly bookkeeping.

use_cases.title: Small business receipt management
use_cases.description: Business owners photograph and forward receipts on the go, with Dext extracting and publishing the data into their accounting software.

use_cases.title: E-commerce seller bookkeeping
use_cases.description: Dext Commerce automatically imports sales and fee data from marketplaces and payment processors for online sellers.

features: Full feature access for evaluation period

enrichment.short_description: Dgraph is an open-source, horizontally scalable, distributed graph database that supports GraphQL and its native DQL query language for low-latency queries at scale.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free/Open Source with paid Cloud tiers
enrichment.seo_title: Dgraph Review 2026: Open-Source Graph Database & Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dgraph is an open-source distributed graph database with native GraphQL support. Explore its architecture, self-hosted licensing, and Dgraph Cloud pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dgraph is an open-source, natively distributed graph database designed to run low-latency, high-throughput queries at large scale using either GraphQL or its own query language, DQL (Dgraph Query Language). It was created in July 2015 by Manish Rai Jain, an engineer who previously worked at Google, where he led a project to unify data structures for web-search serving using a graph-based backend. Jain founded Dgraph Labs to bring that approach to the open-source world, releasing the first version, v0.1, in December 2015 and reaching a 1.0 release by December 2017. Dgraph is written in Go and is distributed under an open-source license (the core historically Apache 2.0, with later editions moving portions to a source-available license as the company introduced commercial tiers), positioning it as a database that organizations can self-host for free while paying for a managed cloud offering or enterprise features if they want additional convenience, security, and support.

Architecturally, Dgraph is built to be horizontally scalable and natively distributed from the ground up, rather than being a graph layer bolted onto a relational or document store. It shards data automatically across a cluster, replicates it for fault tolerance, and is designed so that adding machines increases both storage and query throughput roughly linearly. This distinguishes it from single-node graph databases like the community edition of Neo4j, and from multi-model databases such as ArangoDB or Amazon Neptune that added graph capabilities to broader platforms. Dgraph's native support for GraphQL as a first-class query language (in addition to DQL) was also a differentiator when it launched, letting developers use a GraphQL schema and API directly against the database without an intermediate GraphQL server layer.

Dgraph Labs raised a total of roughly $24 million in funding across multiple rounds, starting with a $1.1 million seed round in May 2016 co-led by Bain Capital Ventures and Blackbird Ventures, with participation from Atlassian co-founder Mike Cannon-Brookes. This was followed by additional seed and Series A funding from Bain Capital Ventures, Blackbird Ventures, AirTree, and others, and a later $6 million seed re-financing round in mid-2022 led by Venrock and Uncorrelated Ventures as the company pivoted parts of its focus. Manish Jain served as founder and CEO from the company's start in 2016 until stepping back from that role around January 2022, having grown the team from a single founder to more than 50 people distributed across the US, India, and elsewhere. The open-source project built a substantial community following, becoming one of the most-starred graph databases on GitHub with tens of thousands of stars and reporting well over ten million Docker Hub pulls of its container images.

Commercially, Dgraph operates (or operated, prior to acquisition) on an open-core model: the core database remains free to download, self-host, and use without licensing fees, while revenue came from Dgraph Enterprise (additional security, access control, and support for self-managed deployments) and Dgraph Cloud, a fully managed hosting service. Dgraph Cloud offers a free-forever tier for small projects and experimentation, a Shared tier priced around $39.99 per backend per month with a data-transfer allowance and per-gigabyte overage pricing, and a Dedicated tier starting around $199 per month for isolated, multi-availability-zone clusters hosted on AWS, GCP, or Azure with stronger performance guarantees. This tiered structure lets a solo developer prototype for free, a small team run a low-traffic production graph on the Shared tier, and larger organizations run dedicated infrastructure once they need guaranteed capacity or compliance controls.

Dgraph's target users are engineering teams building applications with highly connected data, such as social graphs, recommendation engines, knowledge graphs, fraud-detection systems, content-management relationships, and identity/permission graphs, where deeply nested relationship queries would be slow or awkward to express in SQL joins. Its primary competitors include Neo4j (the most widely known graph database), Amazon Neptune, ArangoDB, TigerGraph, and JanusGraph, as well as newer entrants in the graph-and-vector database space that have emerged as retrieval-augmented AI applications have driven fresh interest in structured relationship data. Dgraph differentiated itself on native distribution, GraphQL-first design, and open-source licensing at a time when many competing graph databases were either single-node or fully proprietary, and its early technical writing and benchmarks around consistent, linearizable distributed transactions in a graph context were influential within the broader database engineering community.

Ownership of the underlying technology and team has changed since the company's founding. Dgraph Labs was acquired by Hypermode, Inc. in 2023, a move that repositioned the Dgraph database as infrastructure within a broader AI-application platform rather than as a standalone commercial product line. In October 2025, the combined Hypermode/Dgraph entity was itself acquired by Istari Digital. Despite these ownership changes, the open-source Dgraph database itself has continued to be maintained and used by its existing self-hosted community, and documentation, source code, and Docker images remain publicly available, meaning organizations already running Dgraph in production are not directly dependent on the commercial cloud service continuing to operate under any particular corporate parent.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dgraph is an open-source, natively distributed graph database built in Go, designed for low-latency queries over highly connected data using either GraphQL or its native DQL query language.

Founded in 2015 by ex-Google engineer Manish Rai Jain, Dgraph became one of the most-starred graph databases on GitHub, and is now maintained under Istari Digital following acquisitions by Hypermode in 2023 and Istari in 2025.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Dgraph shards and replicates data automatically across a cluster, scaling storage and query throughput roughly linearly as nodes are added, unlike single-node graph databases.
It supports GraphQL as a first-class API alongside its own DQL query language, letting developers query a graph directly without a separate GraphQL translation layer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Dgraph database is free and open-source to self-host with no licensing fees.

Dgraph Cloud offers a free-forever tier, a Shared tier around $39.99/month per backend, and a Dedicated tier starting around $199/month for isolated multi-availability-zone clusters on AWS, GCP, or Azure.

features.title: Native distribution and sharding
features.description: Automatically shards and replicates data across a cluster for horizontal scalability and fault tolerance.

features.title: GraphQL-native querying
features.description: Supports GraphQL as a first-class query interface directly against the database, without a translation layer.

features.title: DQL query language
features.description: Offers its own Dgraph Query Language for complex graph traversals beyond what GraphQL alone can express.

features.title: Open-source self-hosting
features.description: The core database can be downloaded and self-hosted for free under an open-source license.

features.title: Dgraph Cloud managed hosting
features.description: A fully managed cloud service with free, shared, and dedicated tiers removes operational overhead.

features.title: ACID transactions
features.description: Supports distributed ACID-compliant transactions across the cluster for data consistency.

features.title: Go-based architecture
features.description: Built in Go for performance and concurrency, with Docker images used over ten million times.

features.title: Enterprise security add-ons
features.description: Dgraph Enterprise adds access control, security, and dedicated support for self-managed deployments.

pros: Free and open-source core with no licensing cost for self-hosting
Natively distributed architecture scales linearly with added nodes
First-class GraphQL support simplifies application development
Strong open-source community with an active GitHub project
cons: Ownership changes (Hypermode acquisition in 2023, Istari Digital in 2025) create uncertainty about long-term commercial roadmap
Smaller ecosystem and talent pool compared to Neo4j
Managed Cloud pricing can add up for high-data-transfer workloads
Steeper learning curve for teams unfamiliar with graph query languages

faqs.question: Is Dgraph free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Dgraph database is free and open-source to self-host; paid tiers only apply to the managed Dgraph Cloud service or enterprise add-ons.

faqs.question: What query languages does Dgraph support?
faqs.answer: Dgraph supports GraphQL natively as well as its own query language called DQL (Dgraph Query Language).

faqs.question: Who created Dgraph?
faqs.answer: Dgraph was created in 2015 by Manish Rai Jain, a former Google engineer, through the company Dgraph Labs.

faqs.question: How much does Dgraph Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Dgraph Cloud offers a free tier, a Shared tier around $39.99/month per backend, and a Dedicated tier starting around $199/month.

faqs.question: Who owns Dgraph now?
faqs.answer: Dgraph Labs was acquired by Hypermode in 2023, and the combined company was acquired by Istari Digital in October 2025.

faqs.question: What is Dgraph used for?
faqs.answer: Dgraph is used for applications with highly connected data such as social graphs, recommendation engines, knowledge graphs, and fraud detection systems.

use_cases.title: Knowledge graph applications
use_cases.description: Teams model deeply interconnected entities and relationships that are cumbersome to query with SQL joins.

use_cases.title: Recommendation and social graph systems
use_cases.description: Applications that need fast traversal of user, content, and interaction relationships use Dgraph for low-latency graph queries.
use_cases.audience: Product engineering teams at consumer and social platforms

use_cases.title: Managed graph database hosting
use_cases.description: Teams that want graph database capability without operating infrastructure use Dgraph Cloud's shared or dedicated tiers.

enrichment.short_description: Diagrams.net (formerly draw.io) is a free, open-source diagramming tool for flowcharts, UML, network diagrams, and mind maps, usable online or offline with no account required.
enrichment.seo_title: Diagrams.net Review 2026: Free Draw.io Diagramming Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Diagrams.net (draw.io) is a free, open-source diagram editor for flowcharts, UML, and network diagrams. See features, integrations, and pricing for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Diagrams.net, widely known by its longtime name draw.io, is a free and open-source diagramming and whiteboarding application used to create flowcharts, UML diagrams, network diagrams, floor plans, org charts, mind maps, and general-purpose visual documentation. Its origins trace back to 2000, when Swiss software engineer Gaudenz Alder began developing a Java-based graph visualization library called JGraph as part of a master's thesis at ETH Zurich. JGraph was publicly released in May 2002 and grew steadily in popularity through the 2000s; Alder was working on it full-time by late 2004. In 2006, the team released mxGraph, a JavaScript/HTML successor technology that supported browsers of the era including Firefox 1.5 and Internet Explorer 5.5. In 2011, the company launched a hosted diagramming web application under the brand Diagramly at the domain diagram.ly, and in 2012, after removing Java-applet dependencies, the product was rebranded as draw.io. The underlying corporate entity, originally incorporated in 2000 as Pimuzar Limited for developers David Benson and colleagues, was renamed JGraph Ltd in August 2004, and most recently renamed draw.io Ltd in September 2025.

In February 2020, citing brand-security concerns around the generic '.io' domain and third-party confusion, the company announced it was splitting its public presence: the marketing and documentation site would move to the drawio.com domain, while the actual reference web application would be hosted at diagrams.net, which is now the primary consumer-facing name for the product even though 'draw.io' remains in extremely common use and the GitHub project is still named jgraph/drawio. The tool is a client-side, browser-based JavaScript application, meaning diagrams can be created and edited without sending data to a remote server unless the user explicitly chooses a cloud storage integration; this client-side, no-account-required architecture is one of the product's most distinctive selling points, particularly for security-conscious teams such as government agencies, engineering departments, and enterprises that require diagrams to never leave local infrastructure.

Diagrams.net's core editor is licensed under Apache 2.0, and the standard web and desktop applications are entirely free to use, with no artificial feature gating, no per-user seat fees, and no enterprise tier required simply to get single sign-on or advanced diagram types on the base product. The application can be used directly at app.diagrams.net in a browser, downloaded as a cross-platform Electron-based desktop app for Windows, macOS, and Linux for fully offline use, or embedded inside other platforms. It integrates natively with cloud storage and collaboration tools including Google Drive, Dropbox, OneDrive, GitHub, and GitLab for file storage, and it can be embedded as a diagramming layer inside Atlassian Confluence and Jira, Notion, Nextcloud, and MediaWiki, among others. Real-time multi-user collaborative editing is supported when diagrams are stored in a connected cloud location that supports concurrent editing.

While the core diagramming application is free for essentially all individual and team use cases, the company generates revenue primarily through paid marketplace integrations, most notably the Confluence and Jira app listings on the Atlassian Marketplace, which are priced per user per month on a sliding scale that decreases as team size grows (for example, roughly $1.70 per user per month for small teams, dropping toward fractions of a cent per user for organizations with tens of thousands of users), plus separate data-center pricing for large on-premise Atlassian deployments that can run into the thousands of dollars per year. An Enterprise tier exists for organizations that want dedicated support, custom deployment assistance, or compliance-related guarantees, priced on request rather than published rate cards. Because the company is not venture-backed and has stated a deliberate preference for sustainable, profitable operation over rapid scaling, its pricing philosophy explicitly avoids the freemium 'artificial scarcity' model used by many SaaS diagramming competitors.

Diagrams.net's principal competitors include Lucidchart, Microsoft Visio, SmartDraw, and Miro (for the whiteboarding use case), and it is frequently recommended as the free, no-login alternative to those paid tools, particularly among software engineers, technical writers, students, and open-source communities that value the Apache-licensed, self-hostable nature of the underlying code. Its shape libraries cover an unusually broad range of technical domains, including AWS, Azure, and GCP cloud architecture icons, network topology symbols, UML and entity-relationship diagram notation, BPMN business-process shapes, and general-purpose office layouts, which makes it popular in software architecture documentation, IT infrastructure diagrams, and technical wikis. The tool's continued active development, large shape-library ecosystem, and permissive licensing have made it one of the most widely embedded diagramming tools in developer-facing software products, including being bundled directly inside platforms like GitHub's Wiki editor extensions and numerous self-hosted wiki and documentation systems.

A further notable fact about diagrams.net's history is how deliberately it has resisted the venture-funded growth playbook common among SaaS diagramming competitors: rather than raising outside capital and layering in seat-based subscription pricing, the company has funded ongoing development through Marketplace app revenue and enterprise support contracts while keeping the core product free indefinitely, a stance the team has publicly framed as a long-term bet on sustainable, profitable software rather than short-term growth metrics. This approach has also made the project unusually stable from a user's perspective, since there is no risk of the free tier disappearing in a pricing overhaul the way it might for a venture-backed competitor under pressure to increase revenue per user.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Diagrams.net, better known by its long-running name draw.io, is a free, open-source diagramming application for flowcharts, UML, network diagrams, and more, usable in-browser or offline with no account required.
Its technology traces back to the JGraph library started in 2000 by Gaudenz Alder, evolving through Diagramly and draw.io before the product settled on the diagrams.net brand in 2020.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The editor offers a drag-and-drop canvas with thousands of shapes covering flowcharts, network diagrams, UML, cloud architecture icons, and more, plus real-time collaborative editing.
It runs entirely client-side in the browser or as a cross-platform desktop app, and integrates with Google Drive, Dropbox, OneDrive, GitHub, Confluence, Jira, and Notion.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core diagrams.net application is completely free to use, with no login required and no artificial feature limits for individuals or teams.
Revenue comes mainly from paid Confluence/Jira Marketplace app listings priced per user per month, plus custom Enterprise pricing for large organizations needing dedicated support.

features.title: No-login browser editor
features.description: Full diagramming functionality at app.diagrams.net without creating an account or signing in.

features.title: Cross-platform desktop app
features.description: Electron-based desktop application for Windows, macOS, and Linux for fully offline diagramming.

features.title: Extensive shape libraries
features.description: Thousands of built-in shapes covering flowcharts, UML, network topology, cloud architecture, and BPMN.

features.title: Cloud storage integrations
features.description: Native integration with Google Drive, Dropbox, OneDrive, GitHub, and GitLab for saving and syncing diagrams.

features.title: Real-time collaboration
features.description: Multiple users can co-edit the same diagram simultaneously when stored in a connected cloud location.

features.title: Confluence and Jira embedding
features.description: Native apps let teams embed and edit diagrams directly inside Atlassian Confluence pages and Jira issues.

features.title: Client-side architecture
features.description: Diagrams are processed locally in the browser rather than sent to a remote server by default, aiding security-sensitive use.

features.title: Apache 2.0 licensed core
features.description: The underlying source code is open and self-hostable, enabling on-premise deployments for compliance needs.

pros: Completely free for individuals and teams with no feature gating
No account or login required to start diagramming
Extremely broad shape library covering technical and business diagram types
Open-source and self-hostable for security-sensitive organizations
cons: Interface feels less polished than premium competitors like Lucidchart
Real-time collaboration depends on which cloud storage backend is connected
Enterprise support and SLAs require custom, quote-based pricing
Confluence/Jira integration costs scale with user count for larger teams

faqs.question: Is diagrams.net the same as draw.io?
faqs.answer: Yes, diagrams.net is the current primary brand name for the tool long known as draw.io; both names are used interchangeably and the software is identical.

faqs.question: Is diagrams.net really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core web and desktop applications are completely free with no feature limits; the company monetizes through optional paid Confluence/Jira Marketplace apps and custom Enterprise support.

faqs.question: Do I need an account to use diagrams.net?
faqs.answer: No, you can use the full editor at app.diagrams.net without signing up, and optionally connect a cloud storage account like Google Drive to save files.

faqs.question: Is diagrams.net open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core editor is released under the Apache 2.0 license and the source code is available on GitHub under jgraph/drawio.

faqs.question: Can diagrams.net be used offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, a desktop app for Windows, macOS, and Linux allows fully offline diagram creation and editing.

faqs.question: What is diagrams.net used for?
faqs.answer: It's used to create flowcharts, UML diagrams, network diagrams, floor plans, org charts, mind maps, and cloud architecture diagrams.

use_cases.title: Software architecture documentation
use_cases.description: Engineers diagram system architecture, network topology, and cloud infrastructure using dedicated AWS, Azure, and GCP shape libraries.

use_cases.title: Business process mapping
use_cases.description: Teams document workflows and BPMN business processes using drag-and-drop flowchart shapes.

use_cases.title: Embedded wiki diagrams
use_cases.description: Organizations embed diagrams.net directly inside Confluence, Notion, or self-hosted wikis for living documentation.

features: All diagram types and shape libraries
Embedded diagram editing in Confluence and Jira
Sliding per-user pricing that decreases at scale

enrichment.short_description: Dialpad is an AI-powered business communications platform offering cloud VoIP calling, video meetings, messaging, and an AI-driven contact center built on Google Cloud.
enrichment.seo_title: Dialpad Review 2026: AI Phone System Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dialpad is an AI-powered business phone, meetings, and contact center platform. Compare 2026 pricing plans, features, pros, cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dialpad is a cloud-based, AI-powered business communications platform that combines VoIP phone service, video conferencing, team messaging, and an AI-driven contact center into a single unified system. The company was founded in 2011 by Craig Walker, John Rector, and Brian Peterson, and is headquartered in San Ramon, California. Craig Walker had previously founded GrandCentral (acquired by Google and turned into Google Voice) and later led Google Voice and Yahoo! Voice, giving the founding team deep experience in consumer and business voice communications before starting Dialpad. The company originally operated under the name Firespotter Labs and first gained market traction with UberConference, a browser-based conferencing product, before rebranding the overall company to Dialpad and focusing on cloud business telephony and unified communications as its primary product line.

Dialpad has raised approximately $476 million in venture funding across nine rounds since its first raise in May 2011, from investors including Andreessen Horowitz, Google Ventures (GV), T-Mobile, Amasia, and ICONIQ Growth, among others. A notable funding round announced a $170 million raise at a $2.2 billion valuation, positioning the company as a Series F-stage business heading toward or already holding unicorn status. This capital has funded rapid expansion of Dialpad's product line from a single voice product into a broader unified-communications-as-a-service (UCaaS) and contact-center-as-a-service (CCaaS) suite that competes directly with RingCentral, 8x8, Nextiva, Vonage, Zoom Phone, and Five9 in the cloud business-communications market.

Dialpad's core technical differentiator is its deep integration of proprietary artificial intelligence throughout the calling and meetings experience. The platform is built on Google Cloud infrastructure and uses Dialpad's own large language model, referred to as DialpadGPT, alongside other in-house AI models trained specifically on voice and conversational business data, rather than relying solely on third-party AI providers. This AI layer powers real-time call transcription, live sentiment analysis during customer calls, automatic post-call summaries and action-item extraction, real-time coaching prompts for sales and support agents, and voice-based analytics that turn every phone call or meeting into structured, searchable data. Dialpad markets this as turning conversations into 'customer intelligence' that can feed CRM systems, sales coaching workflows, and support quality programs automatically, without requiring a human note-taker.

The product line is organized into several core offerings: Dialpad Ai Voice, the core cloud business phone system with unlimited calling, call routing, voicemail transcription, and AI call summaries; Dialpad Ai Meetings, a video conferencing product with AI-generated meeting notes and action items; Dialpad Ai Contact Center, an AI-enhanced customer support and sales contact center with agent coaching, automated quality management, and AI assist features; and Dialpad Ai Sales, a sales-engagement tool with real-time coaching and deal intelligence. These products can be bundled or purchased somewhat independently depending on a customer's needs, and they share the same underlying AI and administrative platform.

Dialpad's pricing is structured per user per month and varies by product line and billing frequency. For Dialpad Ai Voice, the Standard tier starts around $15 per user per month when billed annually (or roughly $23 per month when billed month-to-month), and the Pro tier is priced around $25 per user per month annually (or about $35 monthly), with an Enterprise tier available at custom, quote-based pricing for large organizations needing advanced security, compliance, and unlimited office locations. Ai Meetings, Ai Contact Center, and Ai Sales have their own separate tiered pricing structures, generally following a similar Standard/Pro/Enterprise pattern, and multi-product bundles are often available at a discount. A free trial is offered for prospective customers to evaluate the platform before committing to a paid plan.

Dialpad's target customers range from small and mid-sized businesses that want a modern, AI-enhanced replacement for a legacy on-premise PBX phone system, to larger enterprises and dedicated contact centers that need AI-driven agent coaching, quality assurance automation, and deep CRM integrations (Salesforce, HubSpot, Zendesk, and others are commonly supported). Because the platform runs entirely in the cloud and offers native desktop and mobile apps for iOS, Android, Windows, and macOS, it appeals particularly to distributed and remote-first teams that need a phone system not tied to physical office hardware. Reviewers on G2 and Capterra frequently highlight the quality and usefulness of Dialpad's AI transcription and coaching features as a standout differentiator versus more traditional VoIP competitors, while some reviews note that advanced AI features and larger user counts increase the cost relative to bare-bones VoIP alternatives, and that call quality can occasionally depend on the customer's own internet connection quality since the system is entirely cloud-based rather than using dedicated telephony hardware.

Dialpad has also continued expanding its enterprise footprint through acquisitions and partnerships that broaden its AI and data capabilities, and it regularly appears in analyst evaluations of the UCaaS and CCaaS markets alongside larger, longer-established communications vendors. Its emphasis on training proprietary voice and language models in-house, rather than exclusively wrapping a third-party model, is frequently cited by the company as the reason its transcription and coaching features can be tuned specifically for phone and meeting audio conditions, including handling accents, cross-talk, and background noise more reliably than general-purpose transcription tools not built specifically around telephony workloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dialpad is a cloud-based business communications platform unifying AI-powered phone calling, video meetings, messaging, and contact center software into one system.
Founded in 2011 by Craig Walker (formerly of Google Voice), John Rector, and Brian Peterson, the San Ramon, California company has raised roughly $476 million and reached a $2.2 billion valuation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Dialpad runs on Google Cloud and uses its own proprietary DialpadGPT model to power real-time transcription, live sentiment analysis, automatic call summaries, and agent coaching.
The product line spans Dialpad Ai Voice for business phone service, Ai Meetings for video conferencing, and Ai Contact Center for AI-enhanced customer support and sales.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Dialpad Ai Voice starts around $15/user/month billed annually (Standard tier) or $23/month billed monthly, with a Pro tier around $25/user/month annually.
An Enterprise tier is available at custom pricing for large organizations, and each product line (Meetings, Contact Center, Sales) has its own separate tiered pricing.

faqs.question: What is Dialpad used for?
faqs.answer: Dialpad is used as a cloud business phone system, video meeting platform, and AI-powered contact center for customer support and sales teams.

faqs.question: How much does Dialpad cost?
faqs.answer: Dialpad Ai Voice starts around $15 per user per month billed annually (Standard tier), with a Pro tier around $25 per user per month annually and custom Enterprise pricing available.

faqs.question: Who founded Dialpad?
faqs.answer: Dialpad was founded in 2011 by Craig Walker, John Rector, and Brian Peterson, originally operating as Firespotter Labs before becoming UberConference and then Dialpad.

faqs.answer: Dialpad uses its own proprietary large language model, DialpadGPT, built on Google Cloud infrastructure, to power transcription, summaries, and coaching.

faqs.answer: Yes, Dialpad offers a free trial for prospective customers to evaluate the platform before purchasing.

faqs.question: Who are Dialpad's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Dialpad competes with RingCentral, 8x8, Nextiva, Vonage, Zoom Phone, and Five9 in the cloud business communications market.

enrichment.short_description: diaspora* is a free, open-source, privacy-focused social network built on a decentralized network of independently run servers called pods.
enrichment.seo_title: diaspora* Review 2026: Free Decentralized Social Network
enrichment.seo_meta_description: diaspora* is a free, open-source, decentralized social network run on independent pods. Learn its history, how it works, privacy model, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
diaspora*, stylized with a trailing asterisk, is a free, open-source, privacy-focused social networking platform built on a decentralized architecture rather than a single corporate-owned server. The project was founded in 2010 by four undergraduate students at New York University's Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences: Ilya Zhitomirskiy, Dan Grippi, Max Salzberg, and Raphael Sofaer. The founders were directly motivated by a February 2010 lecture from Columbia University law professor Eben Moglen, in which he criticized centralized social networks like Facebook as functioning as 'spying for free,' harvesting personal data for corporate and advertising purposes. In response, the students set out to build a social network with no central owner, where users would retain full control and ownership of their own data. To fund initial development, the team ran a Kickstarter campaign that raised more than $200,000, far exceeding its original goal and generating significant press attention as an early example of crowdfunded software development. A consumer alpha version of diaspora* launched on November 23, 2010.

Architecturally, diaspora* is not a single website but a federated protocol-based network made up of independently owned and operated servers called 'pods.' Anyone can run a pod using the open-source diaspora* software, and pods communicate with each other so that a user on one pod can follow, message, and interact with users on entirely different pods, similar in concept to how email works across different providers, or how the later Mastodon network operates in the fediverse. This design means no single company or government can unilaterally shut down or control the entire network; if one pod goes offline or its administrator imposes rules a user dislikes, the user can migrate to another pod without losing the broader network of connections. The software itself is built primarily in Ruby on Rails and is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPL-3.0), meaning anyone can inspect, modify, and redistribute the code, and anyone running a modified version as a network service must also make their modified source available to users.

Key product concepts that differentiate diaspora* from mainstream social networks include 'Aspects,' a mechanism that lets users segment their contacts into groups (such as family, coworkers, or friends) and choose exactly which Aspect sees each post, giving far more granular privacy control than a simple public/private toggle. Because diaspora* is not funded by advertising, the platform has no algorithmic engagement-optimized news feed, no ad targeting, and no data harvesting business model; content is generally shown in reverse-chronological order from accounts a user follows. Community observations have noted that, compared to mainstream social platforms, diaspora*'s traffic skews toward substantive discussion of ideas, technology, and social causes rather than casual daily-life sharing, partly because its early adopters were drawn from privacy-conscious and open-source-oriented communities.

diaspora* has always been a nonprofit, community-governed project rather than a venture-backed company; it has no pricing plans, no subscription tiers, and no company in the traditional sense to acquire or sell it. After early tragedy struck the founding team, when co-founder Ilya Zhitomirskiy passed away in 2011, the remaining founders formally handed control of the project to its user and developer community in August 2012, at which point ongoing governance moved to the diaspora* Foundation, which operates under the umbrella of the Free Software Support Network, a US-based nonprofit. Major and minor releases since then have been community-driven, with the first fully community-led release, version 0.0.1.0, shipping in October 2012. Development has continued steadily since, including a version 0.9.0.0 release in mid-2024 that introduced an API layer enabling third-party and mobile client applications to connect to diaspora* pods, and a 0.9.1.0 release in early 2026. Governance decisions are made democratically using the Loomio deliberation and voting platform, reflecting the project's broader commitment to transparent, non-hierarchical decision-making.

As of recent counts, diaspora* has more than 859,000 registered user accounts spread across its network of independently run pods, though, like most federated social networks, active daily usage is concentrated on a smaller number of well-maintained pods such as the long-running joindiaspora.com and other community favorites. diaspora*'s closest conceptual competitors are other members of the 'fediverse' of federated, open-source social platforms, most notably Mastodon (a Twitter-like microblogging alternative that in fact borrowed some federation concepts diaspora* pioneered), Friendica, and Pleroma, as well as broader decentralized-web projects like Nostr. diaspora* is historically notable as one of the earliest mainstream attempts at a federated, user-owned alternative to centralized social media, predating Mastodon's 2016 launch by several years and helping popularize the architectural pattern that the modern fediverse now relies on.

Because it predates most of today's fediverse tooling, diaspora* also influenced the technical conversation around federation protocols; while modern platforms like Mastodon converged on the ActivityPub standard, diaspora* originally used its own custom federation protocol before later adding partial interoperability efforts with the broader fediverse. The project's long survival, now well over fifteen years since its Kickstarter debut, without any corporate owner, advertising revenue, or acquisition, is itself a notable data point in the history of alternative social media, frequently cited by researchers and journalists studying decentralized platforms as an early proof of concept that a socially significant network can be sustained indefinitely by volunteer labor and small donations rather than venture capital or ad sales.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: diaspora* is a free, open-source, decentralized social network made up of independently run servers called pods that interoperate so users can connect across the entire network regardless of which pod they join.
Founded in 2010 by four NYU students after a Kickstarter campaign raised over $200,000, the project is now governed by the nonprofit diaspora* Foundation rather than a corporation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: diaspora*'s 'Aspects' feature lets users group contacts and control exactly which group sees each post, offering more granular privacy than a simple public/private toggle.
The platform has no advertising, no algorithmic feed, and no data-harvesting business model, showing posts in reverse-chronological order instead.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: diaspora* is entirely free to use; anyone can join an existing pod or run their own pod using the open-source, AGPL-3.0-licensed software.
There are no subscription tiers, premium features, or advertising-based monetization, consistent with its nonprofit, community-governed structure.

features.title: Federated pod network
features.description: Independently run servers called pods interoperate so users on different pods can still follow and interact with each other.

features.title: Aspects-based privacy control
features.description: Users group contacts into Aspects and choose exactly which group sees each individual post.

features.title: No advertising or data harvesting
features.description: The platform has no ad-targeting business model and does not sell or exploit user data.

features.title: Reverse-chronological feed
features.description: Posts appear in chronological order rather than through an engagement-optimized algorithm.

features.title: Open-source, self-hostable software
features.description: Anyone can run their own pod using the publicly available Ruby on Rails codebase.

features.title: Cross-pod interaction
features.description: Users can follow, message, and interact with accounts on entirely different pods, similar to federated email.

features.title: API access for third-party clients
features.description: Since version 0.9.0.0, an API layer supports third-party and mobile client applications.

features.title: Democratic governance
features.description: Project decisions are made transparently through community voting on the Loomio platform.

pros: Completely free with no ads, tracking, or data-harvesting business model
Decentralized architecture means no single company can shut down or control the network
Granular Aspects-based privacy controls exceed typical public/private toggles
Fully open-source, allowing anyone to audit, self-host, or modify the software
cons: Smaller, less active user base compared to mainstream social networks
Choosing and trusting a pod adds friction compared to signing up for a single centralized service
Slower feature development pace as a volunteer-run, non-commercial project
Network effects favor larger platforms, limiting mainstream discovery and growth

faqs.question: What is diaspora*?
faqs.answer: diaspora* is a free, open-source, decentralized social network made up of independently run servers called pods that let users interact across the whole network.

faqs.question: Is diaspora* free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, diaspora* is entirely free, with no subscription plans, advertising, or premium tiers.

faqs.question: Who created diaspora*?
faqs.answer: diaspora* was founded in 2010 by four NYU students: Ilya Zhitomirskiy, Dan Grippi, Max Salzberg, and Raphael Sofaer, after a successful Kickstarter campaign.

faqs.question: What is a 'pod' in diaspora*?
faqs.answer: A pod is an independently owned and operated server running the diaspora* software; pods communicate with each other so users on different pods can still connect.

faqs.question: Who runs diaspora* today?
faqs.answer: diaspora* is governed by the nonprofit diaspora* Foundation, part of the Free Software Support Network, using democratic community decision-making.

faqs.question: How is diaspora* different from Facebook?
faqs.answer: Unlike Facebook, diaspora* has no central owner, no advertising, no algorithmic feed, and gives users granular control over who sees each post via Aspects.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused social networking
use_cases.description: Users who want to avoid advertising-driven, data-harvesting social platforms join or run a diaspora* pod.

use_cases.title: Community and interest-based discussion
use_cases.description: Groups interested in technology, activism, or specific niche topics run dedicated pods for focused community discussion.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted social infrastructure
use_cases.description: Organizations or individuals run their own pod to have full control over their social networking infrastructure and data.

features: Full access to posting, Aspects, and federation
Option to self-host your own pod

enrichment.short_description: Dify is an open-source LLM application development platform combining a visual workflow builder, RAG pipeline, AI agent framework, and model management for building AI apps.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Sandbox); self-hosted Community edition also free
enrichment.seo_title: Dify Review 2026: Open-Source LLM App Platform & Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dify is an open-source platform for building LLM apps, agents, and RAG pipelines with a visual workflow builder. Compare 2026 pricing plans and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dify is an open-source platform for developing large language model (LLM) applications, combining a visual workflow builder, a prompt engineering IDE, a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline, an AI agent framework, model management, and observability tooling into a single system that can be self-hosted or used as a managed cloud service. The platform is developed by LangGenius, Inc., a company founded in 2023 by Luyu Zhang, who serves as founder and chief executive, and John Wang, who serves as co-founder and chief engineer. LangGenius is headquartered in Sunnyvale, California, positioning it within the Silicon Valley AI infrastructure ecosystem, though much of its engineering and open-source community activity has strong ties to the Chinese developer community as well, and the product interface and documentation are available in both English and Chinese.

Dify's core value proposition is letting developers and technical teams go from an idea to a production AI application, such as a customer support chatbot, an internal knowledge-base question-answering system, a multi-step autonomous agent, or a document-processing workflow, without hand-building the underlying orchestration plumbing that connects a large language model to tools, data sources, and business logic. Its visual workflow builder presents a node-based canvas where developers can chain together LLM calls, conditional logic, code-execution nodes, API calls to external tools, and retrieval steps, similar in spirit to tools like n8n or Zapier but purpose-built around LLM-specific primitives like prompt templates, few-shot examples, and RAG retrieval nodes. The RAG pipeline lets teams upload private documents, which Dify automatically chunks, embeds, and indexes so an LLM can answer questions grounded in that private data rather than only its training data. The agent framework supports tool-calling and multi-step reasoning loops, and the platform is model-agnostic, supporting numerous LLM providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, open-weight models via providers like Hugging Face, and self-hosted inference backends, so teams are not locked into a single model vendor.

Dify's underlying source code is publicly available on GitHub under the langgenius/dify repository, released under an open-source license based on Apache 2.0 with some added conditions (commonly referred to as the Dify Open Source License), which permits free self-hosting and modification but restricts certain multi-tenant SaaS resale use cases without a separate commercial agreement, a licensing pattern common among modern open-core AI infrastructure companies seeking to prevent large cloud providers from directly reselling their software as a competing hosted service. This makes Dify's Community edition free to self-host indefinitely for internal or product use, while LangGenius monetizes primarily through Dify Cloud, its managed hosting offering, and through Enterprise licensing for organizations that want the platform self-hosted but with commercial support and advanced governance features.

On pricing, Dify Cloud offers a free Sandbox tier including around 200 message credits, one workspace, one team member, up to five apps, fifty knowledge-base documents, and modest storage and API rate limits, suitable for evaluation and small experiments. Paid cloud tiers include a Professional plan priced around $59 per month (or roughly $590 per year when billed annually, reflecting a discount versus monthly billing) that raises limits to thousands of monthly message credits, three team members, fifty apps, and higher document and storage allowances with no API rate limiting, and a Team plan priced around $159 per month (or about $1,590 annually) that further raises limits to fifty team members, two hundred apps, and larger knowledge-base and storage capacity. Above these, an Enterprise tier offers custom, quote-based pricing covering multiple workspaces, single sign-on, advanced security controls, dedicated support, and a commercial license suitable for organizations that need Dify self-hosted at scale with vendor support rather than running the bare open-source Community edition unsupported.

LangGenius has attracted institutional investment reflecting strong demand for LLM application infrastructure: reports indicate the company raised a Series Pre-A round of roughly $30 million, led by HSG (Hongshan, the investment firm that succeeded Sequoia Capital China), with participation from GL Ventures, Alt-Alpha Capital, 5Y Capital, Mizuho Leaguer Investment, and NYX Ventures, at a reported valuation in the range of $180 million. This funding places Dify among the better-capitalized players in the fast-growing category of LLM orchestration and agent-building platforms.

Dify's target users span AI application developers at startups and enterprises, internal platform and data teams building RAG-based knowledge assistants, no-code and low-code builders who want to prototype LLM-powered products without deep machine-learning expertise, and independent developers building AI SaaS products on top of Dify's orchestration layer. Its principal competitors include LangChain and LangFlow (open-source LLM orchestration frameworks and visual builders), Flowise, n8n's AI-specific workflow nodes, Microsoft's Copilot Studio, and hosted agent-building platforms from major cloud vendors. Dify differentiates itself through its combination of an accessible visual builder aimed at both developers and less technical team members, a genuinely open-source and self-hostable core, model-agnostic flexibility across LLM providers, and an integrated RAG and agent stack that reduces the number of separate tools a team needs to stitch together to ship a production LLM application.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dify is an open-source LLM application development platform that combines a visual workflow builder, RAG pipeline, AI agent framework, and model management in one self-hostable or cloud-hosted system.
Built by LangGenius, Inc., founded in 2023 by Luyu Zhang and John Wang in Sunnyvale, California, Dify has raised roughly $30 million in Series Pre-A funding led by HSG.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: A node-based visual workflow builder lets developers chain LLM calls, conditional logic, code execution, API calls, and retrieval steps without hand-building orchestration plumbing.

Its RAG pipeline automatically chunks, embeds, and indexes private documents so LLM responses can be grounded in an organization's own data, and the platform is model-agnostic across major LLM providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Dify Cloud offers a free Sandbox tier, a Professional plan around $59/month, and a Team plan around $159/month, each with rising message-credit, app, and storage limits.
The Community edition is free and open-source to self-host indefinitely, and an Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with SSO, advanced security, and dedicated support.

features.title: Visual workflow builder
features.description: Node-based canvas for chaining LLM calls, conditional logic, tool calls, and retrieval steps into production AI workflows.

features.title: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)
features.description: Automatically chunks, embeds, and indexes private documents so LLM answers are grounded in an organization's own data.

features.title: AI agent framework
features.description: Supports tool-calling and multi-step reasoning loops for building autonomous AI agents.

features.title: Model-agnostic LLM support
features.description: Works with OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, open-weight models, and self-hosted inference backends.

features.title: Prompt engineering IDE
features.description: A dedicated environment for developing, testing, and iterating on prompts and few-shot examples.

features.title: Self-hosted Community edition
features.description: The open-source core can be self-hosted for free indefinitely under the Dify Open Source License.

features.title: Observability and analytics
features.description: Built-in logging and monitoring tools track app usage, message volume, and performance.

features.title: Enterprise governance
features.description: SSO, multiple workspaces, and advanced security controls available on the Enterprise tier.

pros: Genuinely open-source and self-hostable core, avoiding vendor lock-in
Model-agnostic support across all major LLM providers
Integrated RAG and agent stack reduces the need to stitch together separate tools
Accessible visual builder usable by both developers and less technical team members
cons: Cloud plan message-credit limits can be restrictive for high-volume production apps
Open-source license includes restrictions on certain multi-tenant SaaS resale use cases
Enterprise-grade governance features require custom, quote-based pricing
Younger company (founded 2023) with a smaller support ecosystem than established players like LangChain

faqs.question: What is Dify used for?
faqs.answer: Dify is used to build LLM-powered applications such as chatbots, RAG knowledge assistants, and multi-step AI agents using a visual workflow builder.

faqs.question: Is Dify free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Dify offers a free Sandbox cloud tier and a free, open-source Community edition that can be self-hosted indefinitely; paid Professional, Team, and Enterprise tiers unlock higher limits and support.

faqs.question: Who created Dify?
faqs.answer: Dify is developed by LangGenius, Inc., founded in 2023 by Luyu Zhang and John Wang, headquartered in Sunnyvale, California.

faqs.question: Is Dify open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Dify's core is open-source on GitHub under a license based on Apache 2.0 with added conditions restricting certain multi-tenant SaaS resale without a commercial agreement.

faqs.question: Which LLM providers does Dify support?
faqs.answer: Dify is model-agnostic and supports OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, open-weight models, and self-hosted inference backends.

faqs.question: How much does Dify Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Dify Cloud's Professional plan is around $59/month and the Team plan is around $159/month, in addition to a free Sandbox tier and custom Enterprise pricing.

use_cases.title: Internal knowledge-base chatbots
use_cases.description: Teams build RAG-based assistants that answer questions grounded in private company documents.

use_cases.title: Customer support automation
use_cases.description: Companies build AI agents and chatbots that handle customer inquiries using Dify's workflow builder.

use_cases.title: AI SaaS product development
use_cases.description: Independent developers and startups build and ship AI-powered SaaS products on top of Dify's orchestration layer.

enrichment.short_description: Digiboard is a free, open-source collaborative whiteboard by La Digitale, letting teachers and teams co-create visual boards in real time without needing an account.
enrichment.seo_title: Digiboard Review 2026: Free Collaborative Whiteboard Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Digiboard by La Digitale is a free, open-source collaborative whiteboard for classrooms and teams. See features, licensing, and how it compares to Miro.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Digiboard is a free, open-source collaborative whiteboard application developed and maintained by La Digitale, a French educational technology project created by Emmanuel Zimmert. Zimmert, a self-taught web developer and former corporate trainer, launched La Digitale around March 2020 and officially released it in November 2020, motivated by the shift to remote and hybrid teaching during the COVID-19 pandemic and a broader desire to give teachers digital tools they could trust that were not built around advertising, data harvesting, or the freemium bait-and-switch pricing common among commercial edtech vendors. Rather than a venture-funded startup, La Digitale is best understood as a mission-driven, largely one-person-led open-source initiative that has since grown into a suite of more than fifty free web applications used heavily across French-speaking schools and increasingly by educators internationally, with the interface and documentation localized into French, English, and Italian. The project is funded through voluntary crowdfunding and donations from users, which cover hosting costs, and it is operated from Cusset, France, with additional presence noted in Copenhagen, Denmark, and hosted on infrastructure provided by the Swiss hosting company Infomaniak, which the project highlights for its use of renewable energy.

Digiboard specifically provides a real-time, multi-user collaborative whiteboard, often called a 'mur collaboratif' or collaborative wall in French edtech contexts, where a teacher, trainer, or team lead can create a shared digital board that multiple participants edit simultaneously from computers, tablets, or smartphones. Common use cases include brainstorming sessions, live classroom activities where students post ideas or answers in real time, collaborative mind-mapping exercises, and remote-meeting facilitation, positioning Digiboard as a lightweight, education-oriented alternative to commercial whiteboard tools like Miro, Padlet, or Jamboard (which Google discontinued). Users can typically access and contribute to a board without creating a personal account, which is a deliberate design choice across La Digitale's tools to minimize friction and avoid any need to collect personal data from students, an important consideration for schools operating under strict student-privacy regulations in France and the broader EU.

Technically, Digiboard is built as a modern JavaScript web application using the Vue.js framework on the frontend, rendered through a Vite-based build system with server-side rendering support, and backed by a Node.js server using the Express framework, with Redis used as the primary data store for board state and real-time synchronization between connected participants. The application supports configurable file-upload size limits, integrates with the Pixabay API for in-app image search so users can quickly add visuals to a board without leaving the app, and includes configurable session-duration settings that determine how long a given board remains active and editable. The full source code is published on Codeberg (a community-run, non-corporate alternative to GitHub favored by many open-source and privacy-focused projects) under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3), with a small number of bundled assets, such as Google's Material Icons and the Mona Sans Expanded font, carried under their own separate open licenses (Apache 2.0 and the SIL Open Font License respectively).

Because the source code is openly published, technically capable users, schools, or IT departments are able to self-host their own instance of Digiboard using npm for local development, PM2 for production process management, or Docker and docker-compose for containerized deployment, giving institutions with strict data-residency or privacy requirements full control over where board data is stored rather than relying on La Digitale's own hosted instance at digiboard.app. This self-hosting option, combined with the AGPLv3 license's requirement that any publicly deployed modified version also share its source code, keeps Digiboard's ecosystem fully transparent and prevents any commercial entity from taking the open code proprietary while offering it as a closed hosted service.

Digiboard has no pricing plans, subscription tiers, or paid feature gates: the tool, like essentially all of La Digitale's applications, is entirely free for teachers, students, and general users. The project sustains itself through voluntary donations rather than selling premium features, which is a defining philosophical stance for La Digitale, explicitly positioned in contrast to commercial edtech tools that start free and later introduce paywalls. Digiboard's target audience is primarily K-12 and higher-education teachers across the French-speaking world (particularly France, Belgium, Switzerland, and Quebec) looking for a simple, privacy-respecting, no-login collaborative whiteboard for classroom use, though its lightweight design and self-hostable, open-source nature also make it usable by any small team or organization that wants a free alternative to commercial whiteboarding SaaS tools without agreeing to a vendor's data-processing terms.

Digiboard is one of more than fifty applications in the broader La Digitale catalog, which also includes tools for flashcards, mind mapping, screen recording, presentations, and interactive step-by-step guides, all sharing the same design philosophy of simplicity, no forced account creation, and open licensing. This suite approach means a school that adopts Digiboard for collaborative whiteboarding often also adopts several sibling La Digitale tools for adjacent classroom needs, reinforcing the project's broader goal of giving educators a coherent, trustworthy alternative to assembling a patchwork of commercial, ad-supported, or data-harvesting edtech products from different vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Digiboard is a free, open-source collaborative whiteboard from La Digitale, a French educational technology project created by Emmanuel Zimmert and launched in 2020.
It lets teachers, trainers, and teams create real-time shared boards that multiple participants can edit simultaneously from computers, tablets, or phones, without needing to create an account.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Digiboard supports real-time multi-user collaboration, configurable file uploads, and integrated Pixabay image search, built on Vue.js, Node.js, Express, and Redis.
The full source code is published under the AGPLv3 license on Codeberg, allowing schools and organizations to self-host their own instance for full data control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Digiboard is completely free with no subscription tiers or paid feature gates, funded through voluntary donations to La Digitale rather than commercial sales.
Its AGPLv3 open-source license also allows free self-hosting for institutions that want full control over where board data is stored.
